problem_summary: 2024 MCM Problem A (continuous): model how a sea lamprey population that can change its sex ratio in response to resource (food) availability affects its ecosystem, identify the advantages/disadvantages of this sex-switching ability, quantify the impact of sex ratio on ecosystem stability, and extend to additional species. This O-award paper ("Magic Sex Ratio") answers all four sub-questions with two coupled models: a spatially-explicit Population Growth Model driven by sex ratio (Model I) and a 3-R Index ecosystem-stability model (Model II).
models_used: Model I — Population Growth Model Induced by Sex Ratio: a system of coupled ODEs (river: plankton P, river lampreys M1/F1) and reaction-diffusion PDEs (ocean: host-fish Hu/Hi, ocean lampreys M2/F2 as functions of time, longitude x, latitude y)
Logistic growth model for plankton in the river: dP/dt = r_P·P(1-P/K_P) - δ_P(M1+F1)

Improved Logistic + Lotka-Volterra-style interspecific terms for host-fish: parasitism splits host into parasitized (Hi) and unparasitized (Hu) compartments — an epidemiology/SIR-flavored compartmental structure (β·Hu·(M2+F2) infection term, (d+α)·Hi mortality)

Reaction-diffusion PDEs with a Laplacian diffusion term D(∂²/∂x²+∂²/∂y²) for spatial spread of host-fish and lampreys in the ocean, plus a chemotaxis-like gradient-tracking term G(∂H/∂x+∂H/∂y) so lampreys move toward higher host density
Sex-logistic (sigmoid) function η(v)=1/(1+e^{-(αv+β)}) mapping larval growth rate v to probability of differentiating female
Empirical exponential fit v = A·e^{μρ}+M relating larval growth rate to plankton density ρ, fit by Least Squares (A=-0.76, μ=-0.02, M=0.67, R²=0.9804)
Migration sub-model using Dirac-delta time pulses δ(t-t1), δ(t-t2) to fire the two annual spring/early-summer migrations (spatial integral ∬M2 dxdy for sea→river migration)
Haversine formula to convert lat/long to spherical distance and abstract the spherical-quadrilateral study region (Gulf of St. Lawrence) into a planar rectangle
Polygynous mating/birth-rate rule: birth rate constrained only by female biomass, R_female=c·N1_female·η, R_male=c·N1_female·(1-η)
Model II — 3-R Index ecosystem-stability model: Resistance, Resilience, Recovery as three dimensionless, bounded [-1,1] indicators computed from a system variable
Shannon diversity index S=-Σ p_i·log(p_i) used as the scalar system variable feeding the 3-R formulas
Finite Volume Method (FVM) to numerically solve the PDE system on a 15×15 lat/long grid with Δx=Δy=0.5°, Δt=1 day
Model expansion to a 5-species food web (adds competing parasite A and prey fish B) with added competition/parasitism/predation terms
key_techniques: Mechanistic causal chain: food density ρ → larval growth rate v (LSQ exp fit) → sex-differentiation probability η (sex-logistic) → birth rate → population dynamics. This makes 'sex ratio responds to resources' an endogenous, derived quantity rather than an imposed parameter
Compartmentalizing the host into parasitized vs unparasitized (SIR-like) to capture that parasitism reduces host density and can drive host extinction (cited Ebert et al. Daphnia experiment)

Counterfactual experimental design: run identical initial conditions with sex-ratio-switching ON vs OFF across four initial male ratios (p=0.56/0.67/0.78/0.82) to isolate the causal effect of the switching ability — directly answering 'what is the effect/advantage'

Stress-testing the system by perturbing parameters to emulate real crises: famine (P→0.75P), human killing (δ1,δ2 → 2δ1,2δ2), fertility suppression (c→0.5c) — maps each MCM sub-question to a concrete numerical experiment
Spatial chemotaxis term (lampreys track host gradient) plus diffusion — going beyond well-mixed ODEs to a genuine spatial PDE, which differentiates the paper from typical Lotka-Volterra entries

Single-variable isolation for the stability study: choosing exogenous-steroid perturbation precisely because it changes ONLY the sex ratio, keeping birth/death rates fixed (Assumption 3) so 3-R indices attribute cleanly to sex ratio

Discovering and reporting a counterintuitive finding — the 'time lag effect': sex ratio is set in juveniles but only affects birth rate once they mature, so the population can nearly go extinct before adjusting

Two-tier sensitivity analysis on host growth rate r_H: a Shannon-index table for r_H increases and an 'ecosystem collapse time' table (488 → >24000 days) for r_H decreases, identifying r_H as the fragile parameter while sex ratio p is robust
why_it_won: "It nails the central modeling judgment of Problem A: it makes sex ratio an endogenous response to resources via a clean, literature-grounded causal chain (food → growth rate → sex-logistic → birth rate) rather than an arbitrary knob. The counterfactual ON-vs-OFF experiment is exactly the right way to answer 'what is the effect of the ability to change sex ratio,' and it produces a crisp, defensible narrative (resistance to harsh environments + the honest 'time lag' downside). It reaches for genuinely advanced and appropriate machinery — reaction-diffusion PDEs with diffusion and host-gradient chemotaxis, an SIR-style parasitized/unparasitized compartment, FVM numerical solution — that signals modeling maturity beyond a basic predator-prey entry, yet every component is biologically motivated and tied to the lamprey's real three-stage life cycle and the actual Gulf of St. Lawrence geography (Haversine). For the stability question it brings an independent, well-justified second model (3-R indices over a Shannon-index system variable) with explicit design axioms (dimensionless, bounded, monotonic), giving a rigorous, quantitative answer. The four sub-questions each map to a concrete experiment, the assumptions are individually justified with citations, and the team is intellectually honest about limitations (sparse data, time-lag near-extinction)."
section_highlights: "Summary lays out the two-model architecture and method names up front (PDE, FVM, 3-R, Shannon) with figure pointers per problem. Two work-flow flowcharts (Fig 2) and a life-history diagram (Fig 3) front-load the structure. Assumptions section gives 5 assumptions EACH with a justification paragraph and a literature citation — notably Assumption 3 (steroids change sex ratio but not birth/death rates) is the keystone that later enables clean single-variable attribution in Model II. A Notations table precedes the math. Model I builds bottom-up: migration sub-model → sex-ratio sub-model (the LSQ fit with R²=0.9804 is shown) → river ODEs → ocean PDEs, with a biomass-flow flowchart (Fig 6) summarizing the full system before solving. Results use paired left/right plots (switch ON vs OFF) at four initial ratios, making the comparison visually immediate. Model II states three explicit indicator-design criteria before deriving the Resistance/Resilience/Recovery formulas, then presents the 3-R-vs-Δp result. Dedicated Model Expansion (5-species food web, Fig 18/19), Sensitivity Analysis (two r_H tables + collapse-time table), and an honest Strengths/Weaknesses section round it out. 20 references, properly formatted, biology-heavy and load-bearing (each parameter/assumption is sourced)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The rendered data tables in this markdown appear to be OCR/extraction approximations of the original plots (round numbers, some inconsistencies, e.g., Fig 11 left initial host=40 vs surrounding 100) — they illustrate trends but the exact numbers should not be trusted as the paper's real outputs

Heavy parameter reliance with thin data: the team itself admits lamprey data were scarce, so many rates (δ1, δ2, β, D, E, G, K_H, etc.) are literature-estimated or guessed, making absolute biomass values weakly grounded — they lean on qualitative trends and counterfactual comparisons to stay defensible
Several key biological/spatial parameters (diffusion D, lamprey diffusion E, tracking coefficient G) are introduced without fitted values or sensitivity coverage; only r_H and p are stress-tested

Migration modeled with idealized Dirac-delta instantaneous pulses (δ(t)=1 only at t=0) — biologically a discrete annual pulse, but the delta formalism is a simplification and the integral/units are loosely specified

Model II's 3-R analysis collapses the whole ecosystem to a single scalar (Shannon index), losing per-species stability information; the 3-R-vs-Δp result is computed at one perturbation time (Day 5115) and a narrow Δp range (0–0.20)
The host equations mix H, Hu, Hi inconsistently in the diffusion/logistic terms (the text even has a garbled restatement of Hi=Hu(x,y,t)), suggesting the compartmental bookkeeping is not fully tight
Model Expansion omits the actual differential equations 'for the sake of space,' so the 5-species claims rest on un-shown formulations
Assumption 2 (ignore temperature/climate) and Assumption 4 (lampreys die immediately after spawning) are strong simplifications that bound the model's realism
reusable_patterns: Endogenous-driver chain pattern: when a problem says a population 'responds to environment,' don't impose the response as a parameter — build a causal chain (environmental signal → measurable physiological rate via a fitted curve → behavioral/demographic outcome via a sigmoid/logistic) so the response emerges from the model

Counterfactual ablation as the answer to 'what is the effect of ability X': run identical initial conditions with capability ON vs OFF across several starting points and compare paired plots — clean, persuasive, directly addresses the question

Map each sub-question to one concrete numerical experiment / parameter perturbation (famine = scale P, hunting = scale death rates, sterilization = scale fecundity c) and report what happens — turns vague prompts into reproducible results
Compartmental (SIR-style) split of a population to encode an interaction mechanism — here parasitized vs unparasitized host to capture parasite-driven host decline

Spatial upgrade kit: take a well-mixed ODE community model to a reaction-diffusion PDE by adding a Laplacian diffusion term, plus a gradient-tracking (chemotaxis) term for directed movement toward resources, then solve with FVM on a discretized lat/long grid (use Haversine to flatten a geographic region into a rectangle)

Build a stability/quality metric from explicit design axioms (dimensionless, bounded [-1,1], monotonic in stability, ecosystem-agnostic) and derive formulas to satisfy them — here Resistance/Resilience/Recovery over a Shannon-index system variable
Use a diversity index (Shannon) as a single scalar 'system variable' to summarize a multi-species community for downstream stability analysis
Single-variable isolation via assumption design: choose a perturbation mechanism (exogenous steroid) precisely because it moves only the variable of interest, so attribution is clean
Two-flavor sensitivity analysis: for the fragile parameter, report both a diversity-index table (perturb up) and a time-to-collapse table (perturb down) to characterize asymmetric system fragility
Justify every assumption inline with a cited reason, and keep biology references load-bearing (each parameter/assumption traceable to a source) — strong signal of rigor to judges
Front-load architecture with workflow flowcharts and present comparative results as side-by-side paired plots for instant visual reading
